The breathing reconstruction failure mode — blur, coverage & splatting

What actually limits the breathing reconstruction, with measured proofs. Companion to 07_respiratory_variants_analysis.html. Snapshot 2026-06-16.

The recon that matters for the goal: the model fed breathing-corrupted inputs (the real-time free-breathing deployment case). This report dissects why that reconstruction is blurry and how to fix it.

TL;DR.
  • The breathing recon is blurry — measurably. It carries only 0.65× the ground truth's high-frequency detail (vs 1.0 = as sharp as GT).
  • ~75% of that blur is the SPLAT renderer, ~25% is the breathing. Even on perfect clean inputs the splat alone already drops sharpness to 0.74× (that's the ~75%); the breathing inputs add the rest down to 0.65×. So breathing does add blur, but the splat is the bigger cause — and the splat is fixable without solving motion.
  • It's blur, not black holes. The cube stays filled (coverage ≈ 0.713); only ~5–6% of tissue voxels are under-covered (the localized dark spots you see), not gaping holes.
  • Black input pixels do NOT contribute — an intensity gate (intensity > 1e-3) zeroes them out of both the value and the coverage. A voxel that loses all its (non-black) input → coverage≈0 → black.
  • Fix, biggest lever first: a learned decoder / 3D UNet refiner on the splat (the ~75% chunk), then better motion correction (the ~25% breathing chunk).

1. The breathing recon is blurry — what causes it (splat vs breathing)

Goal = real-time free-breathing, so the recon under scrutiny is the model fed breathing-corrupted inputs. It's blurry: only 0.65× the ground truth's high-frequency detail (1.0 = as sharp as GT). The sharpness measurement (§4) splits that blur into two causes:

stagesharpness ÷ GTsharpness lostshare of the blur
ground truth1.00——
splat alone (perfect/clean inputs)0.740.26~75%
+ breathing inputs (deployment)0.650.09~25%
So the blur you see is ~75% the splat renderer and ~25% the breathing. The splat smears even with perfect inputs (next two sections show why); the breathing adds a quarter on top by making slices image shifted anatomy that then gets averaged together. Both hurt the deployment recon — but the splat is the larger, motion-independent lever.

(Aside, not the point here: this blur is a pipeline limit, not the resp model being a bad reconstructor — on identical inputs the resp and no-resp models are within ~2% sharpness of each other, and the resp model is more accurate. The model-vs-model comparison lives in 07_respiratory_variants_analysis.html.)

2. How the reconstruction is built — splatting

The model never outputs a volume directly. It outputs, for every input-slice pixel, a 3-D position (world_points = scanner_coords + Δ) in the normalized canonical cube. Those (position, intensity) pairs are then splatted into the voxel grid: each pixel drops its intensity into its 8 surrounding voxels, weighted by how close it is to each (trilinear). Code: vggt/utils/splat.py:splat_to_volume.

volume   = Σ over pixels of (trilinear_weight · intensity)     # numerator
coverage = Σ over pixels of (trilinear_weight)                 # denominator
V_canon  = volume / (coverage + 1e-6)                          # = weighted average
So a voxel's value is the coverage-weighted average of every input pixel that landed near it. The model only chooses where pixels land (via Δ); the splat does the rendering. This is why the splat — not the model — caps how sharp the output can be.

3. How coverage is computed, and whether black voxels contribute

coverage[voxel] = the accumulated trilinear weight at that one voxel — a per-voxel soft count of "how much input landed here" (the denominator above). It can be 0, 0.4, 2.8, … Don't confuse it with the two summary numbers reported to wandb:

  • coverage_frac ≈ 0.713 = the fraction of cube voxels whose coverage > 0 — i.e. what fraction of the box got hit by any input at all. It is not an average coverage value; it's "71% of voxels are non-empty, the rest are empty."
  • coverage_mean = the average coverage[voxel] over voxels (≈ how many input pixels stack per covered voxel) — a different quantity.
  • gt_coverage_frac ≈ 0.69 = fraction of voxels where the target has tissue (V_gt > 1e-3) — a property of the answer key, not the inputs: a heart-in-a-chest fills only ~69% of a rectangular box; the rest is air / zero-padding that should be black.
Do black voxels contribute? No. Before splatting, each input pixel gets a gate splat_weight = (intensity > 1e-3) (training/loss.py:342). A black pixel (zero-padding, or a slice that drifted off-FOV and shows nothing) has weight 0, so it adds to neither the value numerator nor the coverage denominator. Consequently a voxel that receives no non-black input ends up 0 / (0 + 1e-6) ≈ 0 → a true black hole. Black input never dilutes or fills anything.

4. Proof: it's blur, and the splat is the bottleneck

Sharpness here = mean in-plane gradient magnitude over the anatomy bbox (anatomy voxels only), normalized to the ground truth. A value of 1.0 = as sharp as GT; below 1.0 = high-frequency detail lost = blur. If the recon were sharp but mis-placed (a geometry error, not blur), sharpness would stay ≈ 1.0 and the error would show only as edge mismatches. Measured on the resp (v2) and no-resp (v4) models, 12 val samples each (tools/measure_sharpness.py):

modelprotocolrecon ÷ GT sharpnessraw-splat ÷ GTcoverage_fracbbox PSNR
var2 resp_no_tclean0.7360.7540.71930.86
var2 resp_no_tbreathing0.6490.6350.71326.26
var4 noresp_no_tclean0.7510.7540.72030.00
var4 noresp_no_tbreathing0.6410.6350.70523.14
Three reads: (1) rel < 1 everywhere → the recon is genuinely blurrier than GT. (2) Even clean is only ~0.74× → most of the blur is the splat itself, present without any breathing. (3) recon ≈ raw-splat sharpness → the trained model adds essentially no high-freq over the raw splat; it corrects position (PSNR climbs well above identity) but the splat caps detail. Breathing adds a second, smaller blur on top (clean → breathing drop).

5. Blur vs black holes — which dominates here (your dark spots)

Note on what this section proves. coverage is a holes detector — it only tells you whether a voxel received any input (filled vs empty); it says nothing about blur. A voxel can be fully covered and blurry (its contributing pixels disagreed and got averaged). Blur is proven only by the sharpness number in §4 (0.65× GT). This section uses coverage to answer the separate question "are the dark spots actual empty holes?" — and the answer is mostly no.

Black holes require a voxel to receive zero corrected coverage. But coverage_frac ≈ 0.713 ≥ the ~0.69 tissue fraction, i.e. the inputs fill at least as much of the box as the target occupies — so at current amplitudes (~8–24 mm ≈ 1–3 planes at 8 mm spacing) there are no gaping holes; the cube is filled, just blurrily. Holes would emerge with larger motion or sparser input (fewer slices → planes that lose all their corrected coverage). Your dark spots are real but localized: measured per subject, only ~5–6% of tissue voxels are under-covered (low input weight) — concentrated at specific z-planes and tissue edges, not gaping holes. The coverage maps below make this concrete — V_gt, the reconstruction, the coverage (how much input landed in each voxel), and the under-covered tissue (red) where coverage is low and dark/blurry spots appear:

coverage_seq0_sax_t0.png — per-z V_gt / V_canon / coverage / under-covered tissue (resp model, breathing val). Red = tissue voxels with low input coverage = where dark/blurry spots come from.

coverage_seq7_sax_t7.png — per-z V_gt / V_canon / coverage / under-covered tissue (resp model, breathing val). Red = tissue voxels with low input coverage = where dark/blurry spots come from.

Per-method mid-z reconstructions (identity + all 5 models) for context:

midz_seq0_sax_t0.png — per-method mid-bbox-z reconstruction (bbox-PSNR labeled).

midz_seq7_sax_t7.png — per-method mid-bbox-z reconstruction (bbox-PSNR labeled).

6. Next steps (biggest lever first)

  • Add a small 3D UNet refiner on V_canon, with deep supervision on the raw splat. Most of the blur is a deterministic splat smoothing (the clean case proves the detail is recoverable), so learning splat→GT is a low-risk deblur. Keep a loss on the pre-refiner volume so the geometry (point head) stays honest and the refiner only sharpens. Expect a real PSNR/SSIM + sharpness gain. Doubles as the splat-artifact-vs-motion-error ablation.
  • Or replace/augment the splat with a learned decoder (higher ceiling, since the splat is the bottleneck), or use a sharper splat kernel (finer grid / gaussian / learned) and less aggressive coverage division. Run head-to-head against the refiner.
  • Watch for hallucination. Any learned refiner can invent plausible-but-wrong detail (clean ≠ correct). Validate on held-out / out-of-distribution inputs that it sharpens real structure rather than fabricating it.
  • Stress the coverage regime. Re-run this measurement at larger breathing amplitudes and with fewer input slices to find where blur turns into actual black holes — that's the boundary the real-time (sparse, single-frame-per-slice) target will push on.
  • Keep pushing motion correction. The resp model's registration is good but partial (~5 dB below the clean ceiling); more epochs / better Δ shrinks the breathing-specific blur at its source.

Reproduce: tools/measure_sharpness.py (sharpness + coverage), tools/eval_variants_matrix.py (PSNR matrix), tools/render_variants_panels.py (panels), _html/build_failure_mode_report.py (this page). Splat: vggt/utils/splat.py; gate + coverage metrics: training/loss.py.
